entries.title: What a reverse proxy is and where it breaks
entries.body:
A reverse proxy sits between the client and the backends: it forwards requests, hides the internal infrastructure, and often balances load and caches.
Proxy misconfigurations lead to problems:
• Incorrect access control -> reaching internal/protected endpoints.
• Lack of sanitization in proxy_pass / location -> directory traversal, reading other people's files.
• Trusting client headers (X-Forwarded-For, X-Real-IP, True-Client-IP) -> IP spoofing, bypassing address-based restrictions.
Bottom line: unauthorized access, traversal, leakage of internal resources.

entries.title: Tools
entries.body:
yandex/gixy - a static analyzer for Nginx configs.
MegaManSec/Gixy-Next - an actively maintained Python3 fork of gixy.
shiblisec/Kyubi - finding alias-traversal misconfigs in Nginx (off-by-slash).
laluka/bypass-url-parser - tries MANY URL bypasses to reach a 40X-protected page.

entries.title: bypass-url-parser - bypassing a 40X-protected endpoint
entries.body:
bypass-url-parser -u "http://127.0.0.1/juicy_403_endpoint/" -s 8.8.8.8 -d
bypass-url-parser -u /path/urls -t 30 -T 5 -H "Cookie: me_iz=admin" -H "User-agent: test"
bypass-url-parser -R /path/request_file --request-tls -m "mid_paths, end_paths"

entries.title: Spoofing client IP headers
entries.body:
The headers X-Forwarded-For, X-Real-IP, and True-Client-IP are ordinary HTTP headers, so the client can set or override them if it controls part of the traffic path. Especially with a direct connection to the application server or when the proxy does not filter/validate these headers.
Use it to spoof the source IP: bypass an address white-list, access admin panels "only for 127.0.0.1", bypass rate-limit and geo-restrictions.

entries.title: X-Forwarded-For - how it is formed and overridden
entries.body:
X-Forwarded-For is used to determine the source IP of the client connecting to the server through an HTTP proxy or load balancer.
When a client goes through a proxy/balancer, the proxy adds X-Forwarded-For with the client's real IP.
If there are several proxies (the request passes through a chain), each proxy appends the address from which it received the request, separated by a comma.
Nginx can override the header with the client's real IP:
proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $remote_addr;

entries.title: X-Real-IP - a single address instead of a chain
entries.body: X-Real-IP is a custom header, often used by Nginx and some proxies to pass the client's source IP. Unlike the X-Forwarded-For chain, X-Real-IP contains only one IP - the address of the client that connected to the first proxy.

entries.title: True-Client-IP - a header from providers (Akamai)
entries.body: True-Client-IP is a header developed and standardized by some providers, in particular Akamai, to pass the client's source IP through their infrastructure.

entries.title: Off-by-slash - location matching logic
entries.body:
Nginx matches the request URI against location blocks. The presence/absence of a slash in the location changes the matching logic:
• location /app/  -> matches /app/, /app/foo, /app/bar/123, etc.
• location /app   (without a slash) -> matches /app* (i.e. /application, /appfile, etc.)

entries.title: Off-by-slash - the difference between location with and without a slash
Vulnerable alias without a slash -> traversal
Exploiting alias-traversal
entries.body:
Example of a vulnerable configuration: the request /styles../secret.txt resolves to /path/styles/../secret.txt.
Because the location without a slash (/styles) matches /styles.., the slash after the location name is "swallowed", and ../ moves outside the alias.

entries.title: Missing Root Location - the global root leaks out
entries.body:
The directive root /etc/nginx; sets the server's root directory for static files.
If there is no root location / in the config, root applies globally.
A request to /nginx.conf will resolve to /etc/nginx/nginx.conf - that is, it will serve the server config itself.

entries.title: Missing Root Location - vulnerable config
Caddy templates - SSTI via Go templates
entries.body:
A Caddy config with the templates directive renders dynamic content via Go templates. Caddy processes the response string as a template and interpolates any variables (Go template syntax) from the specified request header.
Any content inside curly braces is evaluated, including data from untrusted input. The readFile function is available in Caddy templates, so attacker input makes Caddy actually read a file and insert its content into the HTTP response.

entries.title: Caddy - response with the content of /etc/passwd
entries.body:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Length: 716
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
Server: Caddy
Date: Thu, 24 Jul 2025 08:00:50 GMT

You came from root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/sh
bin:x:1:1:bin:/bin:/sbin/nologin
daemon:x:2:2:daemon:/sbin:/sbin/nologin

rows: List all files in a directory
